questions.text: I find that maintaining a wish list of things I want for myself keeps me motivated.
I find that I am able to sustain my effectiveness in helping roles because I process emotional content intellectually rather than experientially.
I sense when two people are avoiding eye contact with each other even in a small group.
I feel that being of service is the core of my identity.
I experience goodbyes as clean transitions rather than drawn-out emotional events.
I tend to miss the subtle ways that people signal they are on the same side.
I hold the view that my comfort is a lower priority than the comfort of people I care about.
I can tell when a conversation's real audience is not the person being spoken to but someone who is overhearing.
I find that I end up directing the work even when it is not my formal role.
I experience the ebb and flow of life at a volume that never requires me to adjust.
I believe that structured, reasoned approaches to others' distress produce better results than approaches driven by the emotional impact of the moment.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is the first principle of any ethics that takes the reality of human experience seriously.
I believe that recognizing my own worth and acting accordingly is a sign of health.
I hold the view that people should communicate their needs clearly rather than expecting others to guess.
I set up my environment to minimize distractions before starting focused work.
I feel uneasy making decisions that affect others without their explicit buy-in.
I notice that a single disappointing text message can tank my mood for the rest of the evening.
I learn best by jumping in and experimenting rather than following a structured path.
I feel at home rotating representation duties among group members.
I pick up on when someone is strategically agreeing with everyone to avoid being pinned to a position.
I schedule things back-to-back and adjust on the fly if something runs over.
I naturally gravitate toward the role where I can best support others' efforts.
I prepare fallback options before negotiating or making requests.
I feel that my inner life runs at a steady, sustainable pace.
Five-year plans seem pointless to me because so much changes that they quickly become irrelevant.
I set specific, measurable objectives rather than vague aspirations.
I hold the view that the seriousness with which I treat others' suffering is the ultimate measure of the integrity of my moral life.
My reactions to sudden change are swift and visceral.
I hold the view that having more than I need while others have less is inherently imbalanced.
I regularly take initiative in situations where others hesitate.
I believe that the most durable forms of help come from people who can distinguish between caring about someone and being emotionally entangled with their situation.
I find that the moral pull of suffering has been the single most important factor in shaping the person I have become.
I find that I focus on facts and logic rather than reading between the lines.
I experience the sensation of missing someone as a tangible ache, not an abstract thought.
I routinely set ambitious goals for myself and dedicate significant effort to reaching them.
I feel responsible for keeping a group on track toward its goals.
